title: Question (which hereditary classes are poly-χ-bounded?)
context_text: Esperet's conjecture that every $\chi$-bounded hereditary class is poly-$\chi$-bounded was recently disproved by Briański, Davies and Walczak. This motivates the overarching question of characterising which hereditary classes do have a polynomial binding function.
notes: Posed as a guiding open question in the introduction after noting Esperet's conjecture was disproved; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

title: Open problem (poly-χ-bounded for k-multihole with only odd cycles)
statement_text: For any integer $k \geq 1$, is the class of graphs with no $k$-multihole in which all the cycles have odd length polynomially $\chi$-bounded?
context_text: This class is $\chi$-bounded [9], but it contains the class of $\{P_5, C_5\}$-free graphs. The authors state they cannot yet prove it is poly-$\chi$-bounded, referring to [15] for the best current bounds.
notes: Stated as an open limitation in the introduction; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

title: Open problem (poly-χ-bounded for k-multihole with only long cycles)
statement_text: For any integers $k \geq 1$ and $\ell \geq 0$, is the class of graphs with no $k$-multihole in which all the cycles have length at least $\ell$ polynomially $\chi$-bounded?
context_text: This class is $\chi$-bounded for every $\ell \geq 0$ by [10], but the authors state they cannot yet prove it is poly-$\chi$-bounded, for the same reason as the odd-cycle case.
notes: Stated as an open limitation in the introduction; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

title: Informal conjecture (polynomial Gyárfás–Sumner)
statement_text: It might be true that every forest $H$ is good, i.e., the class of $H$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded (strengthening the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture from $\chi$-boundedness to polynomial $\chi$-boundedness).
context_text: Say a forest $H$ is good if the class of $H$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded. This has been proved only for a few simple kinds of tree and some of their disjoint unions; the polynomial strengthening of Gyárfás–Sumner is fully open.
notes: Introduced with 'It might be true that'; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

title: Open problem (disjoint union of good trees)
statement_text: It is not known that if trees $H_1$, $H_2$ are good (i.e., the class of $H_i$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded for $i=1,2$), then the disjoint union of $H_1$ and $H_2$ is also good.
context_text: Trees of diameter three are good [14], but whether disjoint unions of diameter-three trees are good is unknown. Self-isolation provides a sufficient condition: if $H_1$ and $H_2$ are good forests and one of them is self-isolating, their disjoint union is good.
notes: Stated as 'It is not known that …'; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

title: Question (are all graphs self-isolating?)
statement_text: Could it be that all graphs are self-isolating?
context_text: The authors note that they know very few graphs that have the self-isolating property and none that are known not to have it. The only connected graphs known to be self-isolating are complete graphs, paths of arbitrary length, and complete bipartite graphs (proved in the paper and its predecessors).
notes: Posed explicitly as a question in Section 2; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes
